title: Analysis: Japan's First Combat Role in Philippines War Games Signals Major Shift in Regional Security Architecture
detail: Radio Free Asia published analysis on April 8, 2026, examining the strategic significance of Japan's decision to send combat-capable Self-Defense Forces units to Balikatan 2026 — marking the first time Japanese military personnel will carry weapons and engage in combat exercises on Philippine soil since World War II. The analysts noted the move was enabled by the Japan-Philippines Reciprocal Access Agreement (RAA), which entered into force in September 2025, and reflects Japan's accelerating departure from its postwar pacifist security posture under the National Defense Strategy approved in December 2022. The analysis observed that Japan's participation signals a shift from the alliance structure being primarily bilateral (US-Philippines) to a multilateral first-island-chain defense architecture integrating Japan, Australia, and France alongside the US-Philippines alliance. The article highlighted the symbolic weight of the deployment: the last time Japanese troops conducted combat operations in the Philippines was during the brutal 1941–1945 occupation. Philippine analysts cited the participation as reflecting strong national consensus that the geopolitical stakes justify a historic strategic reset with Tokyo.
